response:
The finding `crime_00034` ("Plural Mismatch", low severity) flags two declarations in `src/api/state.ts`. Here's the breakdown for each.

## Finding `crime_00034` — `singular_plural_type_mismatch`

> **Evidence:**
> - `plural name, singular type: users: User @L24`
> - `singular name, array type: invoice: Invoice[] @L27`
> - `v1 detector — type aliases and generic types are silently skipped`

The file is fixture material (line 2: *"This file is fixture material — do not import it from real code"*), so there is no real "intended meaning" baked into callers — both declarations are constructed deliberately to trip the detector. That said, the same call would apply in real code, so here are both directions for each.

---

### 1. `src/api/state.ts:24` — `export const users: User = { id: "u_1", email: "a@b.c" };`

The **name** says "many", the **type** says "one". The literal on the right is a single `{ id, email }` object, which is the strongest hint that the *type* is the truthful side here.

| Option | Change | When it's right |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) rename to match type** ✅ recommended | `export const user: User = { id: "u_1", email: "a@b.c" };` | The value really is one user — which the initializer shows it is. Note that `user: User` at L33 already exists as the negative-control twin, so this collision would need a different singular name (e.g. `defaultUser`, `seedUser`) in real code. |
| (b) change type to match name | `export const users: User[] = [{ id: "u_1", email: "a@b.c" }];` | Only if callers actually iterate `users`. Nothing in this file does, so this is the weaker choice. |

**Intended meaning carrier: the type.** The single-object initializer is concrete evidence; the plural name is the typo.

---

### 2. `src/api/state.ts:27` — `export const invoice: Invoice[] = [];`

The **name** says "one", the **type** says "many". The initializer `[]` is an empty array, so the *type* is again the truthful side.

| Option | Change | When it's right |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) rename to match type** ✅ recommended | `export const invoices: Invoice[] = [];` | The empty-array initializer and the `[]` annotation agree; "invoices" is the natural name for a list. As with case 1, `invoices: Invoice[]` already exists at L34, so a real fix would pick a distinct name (e.g. `pendingInvoices`). |
| (b) change type to match name | `export const invoice: Invoice = { id: "...", totalCents: 0 };` | Only if downstream code reads `invoice.id` / `invoice.totalCents`. Nothing does, so this is wrong here. |

**Intended meaning carrier: the type.** The array literal + array annotation are two pieces of evidence pointing the same way; the singular name is the typo.

---

### Summary

Both mismatches resolve the same way: **trust the type, rename the variable.** The right-hand-side literals (`{…}` vs `[]`) tell you which shape the author actually built — the names are the side that drifted. The detector's note that "type aliases and generic types are silently skipped" doesn't bite here because both annotations are direct interface references.
